coredata.dc:title: Detection of mercury ions (II) based on non-cross-linking aggregation of double-stranded DNA modified gold nanoparticles by resonance Rayleigh scattering method
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Zhong Feng
Song, Wei Wei
Luo, Hong Qun
Li, Nian Bing
coredata.dc:description: This work describes a sensitive approach utilizing non-cross-linking aggregation of double-stranded DNA modified gold nanoparticles (dsDNA-AuNPs) for the detection of mercury ions (Hg2+) by resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) method for the first time. The double-stranded DNA contains a mismatched T–T base pair in the chain terminus, resulting in a flexible DNA tail and preventing the AuNPs from aggregation. Thus, a low RRS signal is obtained. However, in the presence of Hg2+, the non-cross-linking aggregation of dsDNA-AuNPs occurs, due to the Hg2+-mediated coordination of T–Hg2+–T base pair. The aggregation of nanoparticles generates a high RRS value. Particularly, the solution color and ultraviolet–visible absorption barely changed under the same conditions, while it is capable of detecting by RRS method with a low detection limit (0.4nM), which is 1000-fold lower than that of the colorimetric method. The proposed method was successfully applied to the detection of Hg2+ in real samples. The sensitive and selective assay might be inspiring for the development of new detectors for other metal ions or biomolecules.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mercury ion
Gold nanoparticles
Resonance Rayleigh scattering
DNA

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of gold nanoparticles
Characterization of dispersed and aggregated dsDNA-gold nanoparticles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: RRS spectra of the sensing system in the absence and presence of 50nM Hg2+ in PB (0.1M NaNO3, pH 7.4).

(a) Resonance Rayleigh scattering responses of dsDNA-AuNPs in the absence (curve 1) and presence of Hg2+ at different concentrations (2–11: 0, 2.5, 7.5, 12.5, 25.0, 37.5, 50.0, 60.0, 125.0, 250.0, and 1000.0nM). (b) Linear relationship between ΔI RRS and the concentrations of Hg2+ from 2.5 to 60nM (Inset: corresponding relationship between ΔI RRS and the concentrations of Hg2+ from 2.5 to 1000.0nM). (c) Linear relationship between Δλ and the concentrations of Hg2+ from 2.5 to 60nM.
Selectivity of the sensing method towards Hg2+. The concentration of each metal ion was 25.0nM.
Schematic illustration of Hg2+ determination by the RRS method based on non-crosslinking aggregation of double-stranded DNA modified gold nanoparticles induced by the formation of T–Hg2+–T complex.
Analytical results for Hg2+ determination in drinking water samples (n=5).

head.ce:title: Detection of mercury ions (II) based on non-cross-linking aggregation of double-stranded DNA modified gold nanoparticles by resonance Rayleigh scattering method

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work describes a sensitive approach utilizing non-cross-linking aggregation of double-stranded DNA modified gold nanoparticles (dsDNA-AuNPs) for the detection of mercury ions (Hg2+) by resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) method for the first time. The double-stranded DNA contains a mismatched T–T base pair in the chain terminus, resulting in a flexible DNA tail and preventing the AuNPs from aggregation. Thus, a low RRS signal is obtained. However, in the presence of Hg2+, the non-cross-linking aggregation of dsDNA-AuNPs occurs, due to the Hg2+-mediated coordination of T–Hg2+–T base pair. The aggregation of nanoparticles generates a high RRS value. Particularly, the solution color and ultraviolet–visible absorption barely changed under the same conditions, while it is capable of detecting by RRS method with a low detection limit (0.4nM), which is 1000-fold lower than that of the colorimetric method. The proposed method was successfully applied to the detection of Hg2+ in real samples. The sensitive and selective assay might be inspiring for the development of new detectors for other metal ions or biomolecules.

ce:section.ce:para: The water-soluble mercury ion (Hg2+) is an example of widespread environmental pollutants and mercury-mediated toxicity has adverse effects on human brain, liver, kidney, and other organs (Nolan and Lippard, 2008; Zahir et al., 2005). Therefore, these environmental and health problems of Hg2+ have attracted great interest to develop efficient analytical methods for the sensitive and selective detection of trace amounts of Hg2+. Extensive efforts have been exerted for the determination of Hg2+ using various detection techniques (Kim and Bunz, 2006; Othman et al., 2007; Wang and Ding, 2014; Zhu at al., 2014). Particularly, methods based on the finding that Hg2+ ions specifically interacted with thymine bases to form strong and stable T–Hg2+–T structures have been first reported by Akira Ono group (Miyake et al., 2006; Ono and Togashi, 2004). Recently, different strategies have been proposed based on this unique feature by using advanced nanomaterials, such as gold nanoparticles (Kanayama et al., 2011; Lee et al., 2007), conjugated polymers (Lee et al., 2009), carbon quantum dots (Zhang and Chen, 2014), silver nanoclusters (MacLean et al., 2013), magnetic particles (Li et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2014; Zheng et al., 2013), and graphene oxide (Li et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2012). However, the detection limits of most reported methods are above 10nM, which may not be sensitive enough for certain applications. Because the maximum allowable levels of Hg2+ in drinking water is 10nM on the basis of the standard of the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) (Deng et al., 2013), it remains a great challenge to develop a simple and sensitive method for the detection of Hg2+.

Resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) is well known for its sensitivity and simplicity, as a rapid analytical technique, over the past few years (Liu et al., 2001; Luo et al., 2006; Pasternack and Collings, 1995). RRS is a special elastic scattering generated when the wavelength of Rayleigh scattering is located at or close to the molecular absorption band (Stanton et al., 1981). It provides useful information concerning molecular structure, form, size, state of combination, charge distribution, and so on (Liu et al., 2001). The intensity of RRS will rapidly increase if the wavelength of the incident beam is close to that of the absorption band of the aggregated nanoparticles (Cui et al., 2012). Recently, RRS method has been widely applied to detect metal ions (Fu et al., 2012; Jiang et al., 2009; Luo et al., 2014; Yue et al., 2013), surfactants (Wen et al., 2013; Yang et al., 2009), bacteria (Huang and Chen, 2008), and biomacromolecules (Du et al. 2006; Shi et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2011), by numerous researchers and our group. In this work, we report a highly sensitive and selective RRS sensing strategy for the detection of Hg2+ on the basis of the non-crosslinking aggregation of double-stranded DNA modified gold nanoparticles (dsDNA-AuNPs). This method is based on the unique colloidal stability of dsDNA-AuNPs, which depends on the terminal base-pairing at the distal end of dsDNA tethered on the AuNPs surfaces. The non-crosslinking aggregation is formed only in the presence of Hg2+ through T–Hg2+–T coordination chemistry and it causes the enhancement of RRS intensities. Furthermore, what is noteworthy in our work is that the solution color and UV-Vis absorption barely changed under the trace amounts of Hg2+, while it is capable of detecting by RRS method with a detection limit of 0.4nM for Hg2+, which is 1000-fold lower than that obtained from the colorimetric method Takarada and co-workers reported (Kanayama et al., 2011). To our knowledge, we report a sensitive and selective RRS sensing method for the detection of Hg2+ based on non-crosslinking aggregation of dsDNA-AuNPs for the first time.

Tetrachloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O), Hg(NO3)2, MgCl2, CaCl2, NiSO4, Cd(NO3)2, Cu(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2, AgNO3, MnSO4, Fe2(SO4)3, BaCl2, and Pb(NO3)2 (analytical reagent grade) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. Tris (2-carboxyethyl) phosphine (TCEP) was purchased from Sigma (St. Louis, MO). All of the oligonucleotides used in this paper were purchased from Sangon Biotech (shanghai, PR China) Co., Ltd., and used without further purification. The DNA sequences used in this work are listed in Table S1 in the supporting information.

Phosphate buffer (PB, 10mM, pH 7.4) was used to control the acidity. All other chemicals not mentioned here were of analytical reagent grade and were used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2MΩcm) was used to prepare all the solutions in this study.

All the RRS intensities and spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-4500 spectrofluorophotometer (Hitachi Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) with a 1cm×1cm quartz cuvette, and the slit (Ex/Em) was 5nm/5nm; the PMT voltage was 400V. The acidity of the solution was determined on a pHS-3CpH meter (Shanghai Dazhong Analytical Instrument Plant, China). Transmission electron microscope (JEM-100CXII, Japan) was employed to characterize unmodified gold nanoparticles, dsDNA-AuNPs, and dsDNA-AuNPs with Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of gold nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Gold nanoparticles were prepared by citrate reduction of HAuCl4 (Grabar et al., 1995). All glasswares were thoroughly cleaned in aqua regia (HCl:HNO3=3:1), then rinsed with ultrapure water, and oven-dried prior to use. After boiling a 50mL of the 0.01% HAuCl4 solution, 1.8mL of the 1.0% trisodium citrate solution was quickly added with vigorous stirring. The color of the solution changed to deep red in a few seconds and the reduction of HAuCl4 by trisodium citrate was complete after 8min of boiling. After the solution was cooled naturally to room temperature, it was filtered through a 0.22μm microporous membrane filter. The resulting solution of colloidal particles was characterized by an absorption maximum at 518.5nm. The average diameter of the prepared AuNPs was about 12nm as characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Fig. S1, Supporting information).

First, oligonucleotide probes (HS-DNA) were reduced by TCEP for 2h. HS-DNA was grafted onto the AuNPs surface through the formation of Au–S bond by the following procedure described with slight changes (Kanayama et al., 2011; Storhoff et al., 1998). Briefly, 3.5nM activated HS-DNA was incubated with 1mL of AuNPs (prepared above) at room temperature for 16h. The mixture was then dispersed in 10mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) containing 0.1M NaCl by directly adding the corresponding salts into the dispersion for 48h incubation at room temperature. To remove unreacted HS-DNA, the solution was centrifuged at 14,000rpm at about 5°C for 25min, and the precipitate was washed by 0.3mL of 10mM phosphate buffer (0.1M NaNO3, pH 7.4) for three times. Finally, the precipitate was re-dispersed into 1mL of the phosphate buffer to make a stock solution for the following experiments.

The dispersion was mixed with 16μL of 100μM M-DNA solution to form a dsDNA layer on the AuNPs surface. After incubation at room temperature for 10min, the resulting dsDNA-AuNPs dispersion was gained and used for the following experiments. The TEM image of the gold nanoparticles modified by dsDNA is shown in Fig. S2 in Supporting information.

The dsDNA-AuNPs dispersion (15μL) prepared was mixed with 270μL of 10mM PB (pH 7.4), 5μL of ultrapure water or Hg (NO3)2 solution, and 300μL of 2.0M NaNO3 solution. The final concentration of NaNO3 was 1M. After incubation of the mixture at room temperature for 10min, the RRS spectra of the system were recorded by synchronous scanning at λ em=λ ex (i.e., Δλ=0nm) in the wavelength range of 350−800nm. The enhanced RRS intensity (I RRS) was obtained as ΔI RRS=I RRS–I°RRS, where I RRS and I°RRS were the RRS intensity of the system with and without Hg(NO3)2 solution, respectively. The increased wavelength (Δλ) was obtained as Δλ=λ–λ 0, where λ and λ 0 were the wavelengths of the system with and without Hg(NO3)2 solution, respectively.

In this assay, RRS method is employed for monitoring the change of AuNPs. As we know, RRS is sensitive to the molecular structure, form, size, state of combination, charge distribution, and so on. In order to obtain accurate results with good repeatability, the test solution should be dispersed. However, AuNPs with large diameter are easy to precipitate from the dispersed solution by virtue of the formation of large aggregates (Kanayama et al., 2011), resulting in the decrease in stability and repeatability, which cannot meet the standard for RRS assay. Furthermore, when the AuNPs diameter increased, the background signal increased as well, leading to an insensitive assay. Moreover, for the synthesis of gold nanoparticles with smaller diameter (<10nm), templates were often employed to assist the formation of nanoparticles, such as peptide and polymers (Lu and Chen, 2012), which is unfavorable for modification and analysis of Hg2+. Therefore, we choose the classic gold colloid solution with 12nm diameter in our assay to obtain the accurate results and lower background signal.

The working principle of our sensing system is schematically represented in Scheme 1. First, the HS-DNA was grafted onto the surface of AuNPs with an average diameter of 12nm through Au–S bond formation (Fig. S1, Supporting information). Takarada and co-workers have demonstrated that the dsDNA-AuNPs with a T–T mismatch site at the antepenultimate position exhibits the best visual response for Hg2+ detection (Hurst et al. 2008; Kanayama et al., 2011). In this assay, the increase of RRS intensity (∆I RRS) of dsDNA-AuNPs containing different mismatched sites have been evaluated after addition of 50nM Hg2+ in the presence of 1M NaNO3. As shown in Fig. S3, the ∆I RRS of completely complementary dsDNA modified AuNPs (AuNPs-c) shows a low value. Because the electrostatic repulsion between adjacent AuNPs was decreased in the presence of 1M NaNO3, resulting in the aggregation of AuNPs and the increase of the background signal. However, the changes of RRS intensities increased with the T–T mismatch designed from the distal end (AuNPs-1) to the antepenultimate (AuNPs-3) position. This might be that free oligonucleotide sequences expose the negatively charged phosphate backbone to enhance resistance to the ion sheltering effect, preventing the AuNPs from aggregation and decreasing the background signal (Chen et al., 2014). However, the three consecutive base pairs are stable to form double-strand DNA in chain terminus when T–T mismatch was designed at the fourth position from the end (AuNPs-4), resulting in a decrease in the entropic repulsion between the AuNPs (Hurst et al., 2008; Kanayama et al., 2011). In this case, aggregation of AuNPs occurs even in the absence of Hg2+, which is unsuitable for Hg2+ detection. Therefore, the single-base mutant DNA, with a T–T mismatch at the antepenultimate position, was added to the HS-DNA modified AuNPs solution to form a dsDNA layer in this study. In the absence of Hg2+ ions, the dsDNA-AuNPs are stable and dispersed, resulting in a low RRS signal (Scheme 1a). The RRS intensity depends on the particle diameter and the concentration of the aggregate, which is conditioned directly by the Hg2+ concentration. With the addition of Hg2+, the T–Hg2+–T structures are formed in the chain terminus of dsDNA, leading to the aggregation of AuNPs. Accordingly, a remarkable increase of RRS intensity is observed (Scheme 1b). Therefore, the signal changes make it possible to detect Hg2+ by the RRS method. Fig. 1 shows the RRS intensities of the sensing system in the absence (curve a) and presence of 50nM Hg2+ (curve b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of dispersed and aggregated dsDNA-gold nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: A mismatched base pair of T–Hg2+–T is critical to induce the aggregation of dsDNA-AuNPs. To confirm the formation of this structure, UV–vis absorption responses were first utilized to monitor the behavior of dsDNA-AuNPs in the absence and presence of mercury ions. Because of the limited sensitivity of UV–vis spectrophotometer and low concentrations of dsDNA-AuNPs, the intensity of UV–vis absorption was very low and barely changed after the addition of Hg2+ (data not shown). For further confirming the aggregation behavior, the concentrations of dsDNA-AuNPs and Hg2+-dsDNA-AuNPs solution were concentrated to 10-times compared to those of the RRS experiments. In the presence of Hg2+, a sharp decrease in the absorbance at 522nm was observed (Fig. S4, Supporting information), which could confirm the aggregation behavior of gold nanoparticles (Li et al., 2008). In addition, TEM measurements were employed to characterize the morphology of dsDNA-AuNPs in the absence and presence of Hg2+ (Fig. S2, Supporting information). Without mercury ion, the dsDNA-AuNPs appeared as a homogeneous dispersed system with an average diameter of 12nm (Fig. S2a, Supporting information). However, in the presence of Hg2+, dsDNA-AuNPs aggregated to large particles because of the formation of non-cross-linking aggregation (Fig. S2b, Supporting information). These results indicate that mercury ion-induced non-cross-linking aggregation of dsDNA-AuNPs is feasible and this strategy is promising for the detection of Hg2+.
To achieve the best assay performance, the experimental conditions, such as the concentration of NaNO3, reaction temperature, and incubation time were optimized.

We investigated the aggregation behavior of the modified AuNPs by observing the ΔI RRS with various NaNO3 concentrations (Fig. S5, Supporting information). All of the experiments described below were carried out at room temperature (25°C). First, we studied the colloidal stability of the dsDNA-AuNPs without Hg2+. The I°RRS of dsDNA-AuNPs in the absence of Hg2+ did not exhibit significant change until the concentration of NaNO3 was above 2.0M, which indicated that the surface-immobilized dsDNA stabilized the nanoparticle dispersion. In contrast, in the presence of Hg2+, which makes a T–T mismatch form an intermolecular complementary dsDNA layer on the AuNPs surface, the intensity of RRS increased with increasing the concentration of NaNO3, indicating that functionalized AuNPs aggregated in a non-crosslinking manner. When further increasing the concentration of NaNO3, the I°RRS enhanced largely leading to the decrease of ΔI RRS. Thus, the 1.0M NaNO3 was chosen to adjust the concentration of salts.

The reaction temperature of this sensing system was investigated and results are shown in Fig. S6 (Supporting information). Slight changes of the RRS intensity were observed below 25°C, however, with increasing temperature, the I RRS value decreased sharply and the I°RRS value barely changed. The above data indicated that the T–Hg2+–T structure is unstable at high reaction temperature. Thus, our experiments were conducted at room temperature.

The incubation time after the addition of Hg2+ was also studied by RRS. We recorded the values of I°RRS and I RRS every 5min at room temperature for 30min, and the calculated relationship of ΔI RRS vs. time is shown in Fig. S7 (Supporting information). We found that the ΔI RRS increased rapidly in about 10min and then remained stable. Thus, the assay was incubated for 10min at room temperature.

The dsDNA-AuNPs solutions were treated with varying concentrations of Hg2+ from 2.5nM to 1μM, and the corresponding RRS spectra were recorded. As shown in Fig. 2a, under optimum conditions, the RRS intensities enhanced obviously with increasing Hg2+ concentrations. It can be seen that the RRS signal change increased sharply between the Hg2+ concentrations of 2.5 and 60nM and reached a maximum RRS value of 68. Further increasing the Hg2+ concentration up to 1μM did not significantly affect the RRS intensity. To quantify Hg2+, the RRS intensity at the maximum wavelength was plotted to obtain the working curve (Fig. 2b). The increase of RRS intensity (∆I RRS) is linear to Hg2+ concentrations in the range of 2.5–60.0nM. The linear equation is ΔI RRS=0.7809 C Hg 2++3.1091, with a correlation coefficient of 0.9978. The detection limit is calculated to be 0.4nM based on 3σ/slope (where σ is the standard deviation). When Hg2+ concentrations were larger than 60.0nM, the RRS intensity no longer meets the linear enhancement, because the interaction between dsDNA and Hg2+ reached a equilibrium state. It should be noted that the maximum wavelength (λ) was also changed with the addition of Hg2+ (Fig. 2a). The RRS signal appeared at 585.2nm without Hg2+, while the maximum wavelength increased with increasing concentration of Hg2+. It is interesting that the increased wavelength (Δλ) is linear to the Hg2+ concentration in a certain range. Thus, a rapid and sensitive strategy is proposed for detection of Hg2+ based on the relationship between Δλ and Hg2+ concentrations (C Hg). The Δλ values for different concentrations of Hg2+ were recorded and the working curve was shown according to the relationship between Δλ and C Hg (Fig. 2c). The linear equation is determined as Δλ=0.4109 C Hg 2++1.1091, with a correlation coefficient of 0.9894 and 0.76nM detection limit. In contrast with some previously reported approaches, our method displays a higher sensitivity (Table S2, Supporting information). The linear response might not be satisfactory. The low dynamic range might be related to the concentration of probes (dsDNA-AuNPs). Generally, in the presence of mercury ions of a given concentration, the lower the concentration of dsDNA-AuNPs, the larger RRS signal change and thus the higher the sensitivity found, which is illustrated in Fig. S8. On the other hand, the dynamic range of the assay would be narrow when too low of a concentration of probe was used (Dong et al., 2012). Thus, 15μL of dsDNA-AuNPs probe was eventually chosen in the detection of Hg2+ for obtaining the lowest detection limit and the widest dynamic range.

The selectivity of the sensing strategy was also evaluated by monitoring the RRS intensity, challenging with other metal ions, including Cu2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Ag+, Fe3+, and Zn2+. As shown in Fig. 3, Hg2+ produced a significantly higher RRS signal than that caused by other metal ions at 25.0nM, indicating that only Hg2+ could induce the non-crosslinking aggregation of dsDNA-AuNPs. The results also demonstrate that all the other metal ions display slight and negligible interferences for Hg2+ detection in this assay. The high selectivity of the proposed strategy is due to the fact that T–T mismatch exhibits high affinity for mercury ion against other metal ions and the binding force for T–Hg2+–T structure is proved to be stronger compared to that of the Watson–Crick A–T base pair (Zhu et al., 2009).

To demonstrate the reliability and applicability of this sensing method, recovery experiments for drinking water with various concentrations of Hg2+ were carried out. The drinking water samples were obtained in our laboratory from the water tap. The experimental results are summarized in Table 1, from which it can be seen that Hg2+ concentration recoveries of 97.4–100.8% were obtained with the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 4.15–7.21%. The results indicate the reliability of our proposed strategy for Hg2+ determination in practical samples.

In summary, we have developed a simple strategy for the detection of Hg2+ by RRS technique with high selectivity and sensitivity. In the presence of Hg2+, the non-crosslinking aggregations of dsDNA-AuNPs were formed based on mismatched T–Hg2+–T base pairs, which caused the increase of RRS intensities. The concentration of Hg2+ can be determined by the change of the intensity of RRS at room temperature with a detection limit of 0.4nM. Compared to previous methods for detecting Hg2+, our work possesses four remarkable features as follows: (1) this assay is conducted under facile experimental conditions without any sophisticated temperature control equipment. (2) To achieve high selectivity, T–Hg2+–T coordination chemistry is employed for specific detection of mercury ion, thus no masking reagents are required in the detection progress. (3) The high sensitivity is obtained based on RRS method, which is better than that obtained from the colorimetric methods. And the detection limit is below the maximum allowable value defined by the U.S. EPA in drinking water. (4) This method has been successfully applied to drinking water, demonstrating the proposed method works well for real samples. We believe our proposed strategy is versatile and it can be extended to design a variety of sensor for sensitive detection of other metal ions, small molecules, and proteins by employing unique structures, such as C–Ag+–C, A2–coralyne–A2, and aptamers-based incorporations.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21273174 and 20975083) and the Municipal Science Foundation of Chongqing City (No. CSTC–2013jjB00002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2014.10.061.
